card.label: Case Study · Overflow Agency
card.title: Logo-to-website generator — 90% less designer time
card.description: A tool that deterministically assembles a branded header + hero from a client's logo and copy, on Overflow's own system — in minutes, not hours.

hero.title: From a client's logo to a finished, on-brand header + hero — deterministically, in minutes.

section1.title: Every site takes ~3 hours, and the bottleneck is the designer
section1.body: Overflow Agency builds sites for construction trades on its own production system (base.css + components). The problem isn't writing code — it's the designer who, on every project, re-extracts colors from the logo, picks components and lays out the header + hero. Repetitive, slow work that eats time better spent on strategy and copy.

section2.title: AI does the one thing it's genuinely good at: deriving the brand from the logo
section2.body: Instead of asking a model to invent a page, I flip the load. Hand-built templates on Overflow's base.css impose the structure, and the AI extracts a brand system from the logo — in two phases: --brand is the UI color (nav, links), --accent is the decision color (CTA). Contrast is enforced automatically (WCAG). The rest — assembly, variants, render — is deterministic and testable.
section2.metricLeftLabel: AI call per logo (the rest is deterministic)

section3.title: Five steps from logo to shippable code
section3.intro: The pipeline mirrors Overflow's real Miro workflow — from logo upload to an export that drops 1:1 into their hub.

steps.title: Intake
steps.body: Upload the logo (SVG) and client copy: H1, services, contact, social.

steps.title: Brand from logo
steps.body: The system extracts colors and builds tokens in two phases: UI vs CTA. For INSTEC green dominates the logo (7×) but the CTA is blue — the tool catches that.

steps.title: Assembly
steps.body: Header + hero are slot-filled on Overflow's base.css. The output uses their token and class names — not a generic.

steps.title: Variants
steps.body: 4 truly rendered variants per logo (color swaps, form layout) → a visual picker.

steps.title: Export
steps.body: Ready HTML + tokens.css. Drops 1:1 into the hub — no rework.

section4.title: Proof in pixels, not promises
section4.body: The strongest proof is INSTEC side by side with Overflow's live build — regenerated from the logo and brief alone, with no manual designer work. The quality gate passes 7/7 logos: contrast ≥4.5:1, complete slots, one font.

section5.title: What this project taught me
section5.intro: Three things I'll carry into the next tools.

lessons.title: Determinism > magic
lessons.body: AI does the one thing where it beats heuristics (brand from logo). The layout is template-driven and data-driven — testable, not random.

lessons.title: Token names lie
lessons.body: --brand is often the logo/decoration color, while the CTA uses a different token. Without that distinction the output looks almost right but clicks wrong.

lessons.title: Output on their system, not next to it
lessons.body: We generate on Overflow's base.css, with their class names. Zero foreign code = zero rework.

section5.closing: The tool doesn't replace the designer — it gives time back. 90% of repetitive work disappears; craft and strategy remain.

hero.label: Marketing + AI + Strategy
hero.name: Jakub Dzwoniarski
hero.title: I build complete marketing systems with AI - and I started with a shovel on a construction site.

card.label: Case Study
card.title: From a construction site to AI-powered marketing systems
card.description: A journey from zero skills to building complete marketing strategies, sales funnels, and AI applications - in 2 years.

hero.title: I build complete marketing systems with AI - and I started with a shovel on a construction site.

section1.title: Two years ago I had no skills, no education, no plan - just a laptop from 2016
section1.body: Construction work for 4 EUR/h, retail for 4.25 EUR/h. I bounced between technical schools - electronics, hospitality - no prescribed path fit. No high school diploma, no clue about marketing, and my only contact with technology was a decade of gaming on a PC bought in 2016. I still work on that same PC today.

section2.title: 900 PLN in one weekend on Vinted vs a full week's salary - that was the first signal
section2.body: Selling clothes on Vinted, I earned in a weekend what took a full week at my job. It wasn't a "eureka" moment - it was simple math that changed how I think about where to invest my time. I started looking for what I could do better than manual labor.

section3.title: 3,000 failed cold calls taught me that without product-market fit, no amount of effort helps
section3.body: I bought a course for 1,500 PLN from someone who was better at selling dreams than tools. I made 3,000 calls. Return: ~500 PLN.
section3.subtitle: What went wrong
bullets: No product-market fit - I was selling something the market didn't need in that form
No processes or procedures - "call, show up, talk, leave" is not a sales system
Dunning-Kruger effect - my confidence came from ignorance, not competence
section3.closing: That failure cost me months and my reputation. But I learned things no course can teach: that business isn't what's on the surface - it's 10 levels deeper.

section4.title: I stopped doing everything for everyone and started diagnosing systems
section4.body: I wanted to offer every service to every client. SEO, social media, ads, copywriting - everything at once. Results were poor. I began to understand that clients don't have a marketing problem - they have a system problem. Instead of patching things together, I look for root causes, not symptoms.

section5.title: My process starts with asking "why does the client need this" - not what they ordered
section5.intro: When a client comes to me for a website or an ad, I don't start with design. I start with diagnosis.

steps.title: Research
steps.body: Market, competitor, and customer behavior analysis. Master personas, psychographic data, in-depth interviews. I look for market gaps, customer slang, and communication patterns to leverage.

steps.title: Strategy
steps.body: I answer the question: what are we NOT doing. Three priorities per quarter, reverse engineering from the goal. A sales funnel tailored to fit - not a template.

steps.title: Execution
steps.body: Copywriting first, design second, conversion optimization last. Never "build a page in one prompt". Each layer separately, each one verified.

section6.title: AI doesn't replace my thinking - but it lets me carry what normally requires a team
section6.body: I was skeptical about AI. In 2023 I dismissed ChatGPT as a toy. Now I code applications in Claude Code, build sales funnels with AI, and automate processes. The shift didn't come overnight - I tested, got disappointed, came back with open eyes.
section6.body2: My approach: AI does one thing at a time, well, under my control. Mindless delegation without critical thinking is a recipe for failure. Human-in-the-loop always.

section7.title: In 60 days I built complete marketing strategies for two companies from scratch - simultaneously
section7.body: A hunting store and a law firm. Two completely different industries, running in parallel. From competitor analysis, through personas and brand communication, to sales funnels, lead magnets, and channel implementation.
section7.subtitle: What I delivered
bullets: Complete marketing strategies based on deep market research
Sales and marketing funnels with conversion mechanics
Unique value propositions and brand communication
Systems that continue working without me

section8.title: Selected as the best out of 60 candidates - at 20 years old with two years of experience
section8.body: 60 people applied for a marketing position. I got the job because the breadth of my knowledge exceeded the average - strategy, sales psychology, technology, and automation combined.
section8.body2: In parallel, I built working AI applications - a legal document anonymizer and a design generator - with zero prior programming experience.

section9.title: What I know now that I didn't know two years ago
section9.intro: Every stage of this journey - from the shovel, through Vinted, cold calls, freelance, to AI - left concrete lessons.

lessons.title: Product-market fit is the foundation
lessons.body: Without it, 3,000 calls equals 500 PLN and a lesson in humility

lessons.title: Diagnose the system, not the symptom
lessons.body: The client says "I want a website" but the problem is 10 levels deeper

lessons.title: AI is a force multiplier, not a thinking replacement
lessons.body: One person with AI can carry what used to require a team - but only if they control the process

lessons.title: Build wide, then go deep
lessons.body: Marketing + sales + strategy + technology is a stack that compounds

lessons.title: Failures teach faster than courses
lessons.body: But only if you extract lessons instead of repeating mistakes

section9.closing: Next step: going deep into technology. Backend, Python, frontend frameworks. The goal - combine marketing, strategy, and technology into a stack that lets me build products, not just promote them.
